title: Base gasket oil leak — chronic weeping
description: The Evo Big Twin base gaskets between the cylinders and crankcases are a known leak point. The gaskets compress over time and thermal cycling causes them to weep oil. Worse on the rear cylinder due to heat. Every Evo will eventually leak here — it's a matter of when, not if.
fix_procedure: 1. Clean the area and verify leak location — base gasket vs rocker box vs cam cover. 2. If base gasket, engine must come apart — pull jugs. 3. Replace with Cometic base gaskets (C9529 for .020 or C9528 for .010). 4. Check cylinder bore while apart. 5. Forum tip: use Hylomar Blue on both sides of the gasket for a better seal. Re-torque heads after 500 miles.

title: Starter clutch failure — grinding on start
description: The starter clutch (also called starter drive or Bendix) wears out, causing a grinding or chattering noise when hitting the starter button. The rollers in the one-way clutch wear flat spots and can't engage properly. More common in high-mileage bikes or those with high-compression kits.
causes: Weak battery makes starter work harder
fix_procedure: 1. Confirm: grinding/chattering when hitting start, especially when hot. 2. Remove primary cover and derby cover. 3. Access starter clutch behind the compensator sprocket. 4. Replace starter clutch assembly (rollers + spring + housing). 5. Forum tip: upgrade to a Big Twin starter clutch from a 1990+ model if running an earlier year — stronger springs. Also check ring gear teeth for damage while apart.

title: Rocker box oil leak — dripping on jugs
description: Rocker box gaskets deteriorate from heat and age, causing oil to drip down the cylinder fins. The Evo rocker boxes use a cork/rubber composite gasket that hardens over time. Very common on high-mileage Evos.
causes: Improper torque on rocker box bolts
fix_procedure: 1. Clean and identify: oil running down from rocker box area, not from head gasket above. 2. Remove rocker boxes (4 bolts each). 3. Clean mating surfaces thoroughly — no gouging. 4. Replace gaskets (James Gasket JGI-17030-84-X is the gold standard). 5. Torque to 15-18 ft-lbs in cross pattern. 6. Forum tip: check rocker box for flatness with a straightedge — if warped, lap on sandpaper on glass. Never use RTV on these — the gasket seals dry.
parts_needed: Rocker box gaskets x2 (James Gasket JGI-17030-84-X)

title: Cam cover leak — oil pooling behind cylinders
description: The cam cover gasket on the Evo sits right behind the rear cylinder in the V of the engine. It's hard to see and harder to get to. Oil pools in the V and drips down. Often noticed as oil on the transmission or dripping on the rear tire.
causes: Overtightened cam cover bolts (strips threads in case)
fix_procedure: 1. Verify leak source — cam cover is between the cylinders behind the rear jug. 2. Remove cam cover (multiple bolts, note positions). 3. Replace gasket. 4. Inspect cam lobes while cover is off — Evo cams wear, especially the exhaust lobe. 5. Forum tip: use Cometic gasket with a light coat of Permatex Aviation Form-A-Gasket on both sides. DO NOT overtighten — the bolts thread into aluminum cases.

title: CV carburetor slide diaphragm tear — hesitation and bog
description: The Keihin CV40 carburetor uses a rubber diaphragm to lift the slide. When the diaphragm tears (even a pinhole), the slide can't lift properly, causing hesitation, bog on acceleration, and poor throttle response. Extremely common on bikes that sit.
fix_procedure: 1. Remove air cleaner and carb top cover (4 screws). 2. Carefully lift slide assembly — the diaphragm is the rubber piece around the slide. 3. Hold diaphragm up to light — look for pinholes or tears. 4. Replace if any damage found (Keihin OEM or Sudco replacement). 5. While apart, check slide needle for wear (brass needle, look for grooves). 6. Forum tip: when reassembling, make sure the diaphragm seats perfectly in the groove — one fold and it'll tear again.

title: Weak charging — old-style stator and regulator
description: Pre-1999 Evos use a 22-amp stator that's marginal for running lights, ignition, and accessories simultaneously. Stock output barely keeps up with demand, especially with added accessories (driving lights, heated grips, phone charger). Battery slowly discharges on long rides.
causes: Stator output insufficient for electrical load
fix_procedure: 1. Test charging: battery should read 13.5-14.5V at 2000 RPM with lights on. 2. If low, test stator AC output (disconnect regulator, check each leg). 3. If stator is weak, upgrade to a 32-amp stator (Cycle Electric CE-32T). 4. Replace regulator with matching unit. 5. Forum tip: the Cycle Electric 32-amp upgrade is the single best electrical mod for an Evo. Makes a night-and-day difference. Also upgrade to LED lighting to reduce draw.

title: Ignition module failure — intermittent no-start or misfire
description: The Evo uses an electronic ignition module (often called the 'brain box') mounted under the coil. These fail with heat and age, causing intermittent no-start conditions or random misfires. The bike may run fine cold but cut out when hot. Classic heat-soak failure.
causes: Ignition module internal failure from heat
fix_procedure: 1. Classic symptom: runs fine until hot, then starts cutting out or won't restart when hot. Starts fine when cool. 2. Check ignition module connector for corrosion. 3. Test by spraying module with freeze spray when acting up — if it starts working, module is heat-sensitive. 4. Replace module. 5. Forum tip: Dyna 2000i or ThunderMax ignition module is a massive upgrade — gives adjustable timing curve and eliminates the stock module's heat sensitivity entirely. Worth every penny on an Evo.

title: Oil pump check valve failure — wet sumping
description: The Evo oil pump check valve can fail, allowing oil to drain from the tank into the crankcase while the bike sits (wet sumping). When you start the bike, excess oil in the cases gets pumped out through the breather, causing smoking and oil blowing. First start after sitting is the worst.
causes: Bike sitting for extended periods accelerates the problem
fix_procedure: 1. Confirm: check oil level before and after bike sits overnight. If oil tank level drops and crankcase oil level rises, it's wet sumping. 2. Option A: replace oil pump check valve (requires cam cover removal). 3. Option B: replace entire oil pump if high mileage. 4. Forum tip: if the bike doesn't sit for more than a few days, some guys live with mild wet sumping — just let the bike idle for 30 seconds before riding to pump oil back. But if it's bad (a quart or more drains), fix the check valve.

title: Pushrod tube O-ring leaks — oil at the head/cylinder junction
description: The pushrod tubes on the Evo use rubber O-rings where they meet the cylinder heads and crankcase. These O-rings harden and leak over time. Oil appears at the top or bottom of the pushrod tubes. Often the first leak a high-mileage Evo develops.
causes: Pushrod tube O-ring hardening from heat/age
Incorrectly seated O-rings from previous service
fix_procedure: 1. Identify which tube is leaking (4 tubes total — 2 per cylinder). 2. You CAN replace O-rings without pulling the heads if using adjustable pushrod tubes (aftermarket). 3. With stock tubes, you need to pull rocker boxes and compress the tubes. 4. Replace all 8 O-rings while you're in there (4 upper, 4 lower). 5. Forum tip: use James Gasket O-rings and apply a thin coat of Hylomar to the O-rings before installation. The stock O-rings are garbage — James ones last much longer.
parts_needed: Pushrod tube O-ring kit (8 O-rings, James Gasket JGI-17955-83)

title: Voltage regulator overheating — melted connector
description: Evo-era voltage regulators (especially 1989-1999 models) are mounted in poor airflow locations and run hot. The connector between the stator and regulator melts from heat, causing intermittent charging and eventually complete failure. This is a fire hazard if the wiring melts enough.
causes: Regulator overheating from poor mounting location
Connector not rated for current draw
fix_procedure: 1. INSPECT IMMEDIATELY if you smell burning electrical. 2. Check the regulator connector — look for melting, discoloration, or burnt plastic. 3. If connector is melted, cut it off and hard-wire with ring terminals and heat shrink — don't use the stock connector again. 4. Replace regulator with a MOSFET-style unit (Cycle Electric or Accel) — runs much cooler. 5. Relocate regulator to a better airflow location if possible. 6. Forum tip: this is a known fire hazard. Don't ignore a melted connector — hard-wire it properly. The stock Deutsch connector was not designed for this current level.
